text.fullText: February 17, 2026 U.S. Environmental Protection Agency EPA Docket Center, Water Docket (Mail Code 28221T) 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 Min Waban Dan Submitted via Regulations.gov Administrative Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 (EPA, 2026a; EPA, 2026b; Office Regulations.gov, 2026) 523 Ashmun Street RE: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (40 CFR Part 121) – Proposed Rule Sault Ste. Marie To Whom it May Concern: Michigan On behalf of the Sault Ste. Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians, we submit the 49783 following comments regarding the EPA’s proposed rule, “Updating the Water Phone Quality Certification Regulations” (the “Proposed Rule”). The Sault Ste. Marie 906.635.6050 Tribe of Chippewa Indians (Sault Tribe) is a sovereign government with inherent authority and federal trust protections. Sault Tribe citizens exercise treaty- Fax reserved rights throughout the 1836 Ceded Territory, including rights directly tied 906-632-6696 to and dependent upon clean water and healthy aquatic ecosystems. The Government degradation of waters that support these treaty resources undermines both our Services rights and the federal government’s trust responsibility. Membership Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA) is a crucial safeguard established by Services Congress that allows states and authorized tribes to ensure that federally licensed Economic or permitted activities comply with applicable water quality requirements. When Development properly implemented, Section 401 protects not only chemical criteria but also the Commission physical and biological integrity of waters. This integrity is essential for our cultural practices, subsistence, and intergenerational stewardship. EPA’s stated goal of improving clarity and efficiency in Section 401 implementation is understandable. However, as proposed, several provisions would narrow how certifications function in practice and reduce the flexibility that Tribes need to build a complete record, protect designated uses, and uphold treaty responsibilities, especially in complex, place-specific settings like the 1836 Treaty Ceded Territory. In short, a more predictable process is not a win if it becomes predictably under-protective. The Sault Ste. Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians offers the following recommendations to ensure the final rule supports effective water quality protection, respects tribal sovereignty, and maintains Section 401 as a meaningful safeguard rather than a procedural hurdle. Our comments can be broken down into six categories, including: (1) ensuring the scope of Section 401 certification does not create regulatory gaps for indirect and non-point source impacts; (2) preserving the ability of certifying authorities to require site-specific information necessary to assure compliance; (3) aligning the reasonable period of time with consultation timelines and practical data needs; (4) ensuring certification decision requirements do not marginalize Tribal authorities or Indigenous Knowledge systems; (5) providing sufficient time under Section 401(a)(2) for meaningful review of downstream impacts; and (6) preserving Tribal sovereignty by upholding standalone Section 401 pathways and resisting the erosion of Tribal rights. Issue 1. Scope of Certification: Avoid a Regulatory Gap for Indirect and Non‑Point Impacts EPA is proposing to narrow Section 401 review from evaluating whether the overall project will comply with water quality requirements to evaluating whether a point source discharge will comply. The Tribe is concerned that this change could create a larger regulatory gap for common nonpoint and indirect impacts from federally permitted activities, including altered flows, sediment mobilization, temperature changes, and habitat disturbance. Even when there isn’t a clear “pipe discharge” driving the impact, these effects can still degrade the physical and biological integrity of waters and impair their designated uses. Within the 1836 Ceded Territory, these impacts can degrade waters that support treaty resources and interfere with the Tribe’s treaty-reserved rights, regardless of whether they are from a point or indirect source. Requested change: In addition to point source discharges, EPA should be explicit that water quality certifications may include conditions addressing indirect and nonpoint impacts caused by the federally licensed or permitted activity when needed to ensure compliance with applicable water quality requirements, including protection of designated uses and antidegradation. Issue 2. Requests for Certification: Preserve the Ability to Require Site‑Specific Information Needed to Assure Compliance EPA proposes a single defined list of required components for certification requests and would prohibit certifying authorities from requiring additional components. While predictability matters, a fixed checklist can limit the Tribe’s ability to request site‑specific information and data needed to evaluate compliance, particularly for complex projects with localized hydrology, sensitive cultural resources, or treaty rights-related impacts. Requested change: Allow certifying authorities to require additional, water quality‑related information that is (a) published in advance or triggered by objective project categories/locations, (b) tied to water quality requirements and designated uses, and (c) proportional to project risk. Any changes should preserve regulatory authority for certifying authorities to require additional information when necessary to assess whether the proposed activity will meet water quality requirements and protect Tribal waters and resources. Issue 3. Reasonable Period of Time: Align the Rule with Consultation Timelines and Practical Data Needs Section 401 requires certifying authorities to make a decision within a set “reasonable period of time” (no longer than one year); otherwise, the certification is treated as waived. EPA’s proposal would remove certain automatic extensions and would prevent certifying authorities from asking applicants to withdraw and resubmit a request. The Tribe is concerned these changes could force premature approvals or denials when meaningful consultation timelines, seasonal field constraints, or unresolved data gaps make it impossible to develop a complete administrative record, an impact that falls especially hard on tribes with limited staffing compared to larger state agencies and well-resourced applicants. While forward progress is a reasonable expectation, decisions that have the potential to impact treaty resources via impacts of water quality should be given the amount of time necessary to come to sound science-based decisions and should not be based on arbitrary timelines. Requested change: Restore limited, clearly defined options to pause or extend the decision period for specific circumstances (such as major disruptive events, required public notice steps, or documented consultation needs), and allow withdrawal/resubmission when initiated by the applicant or when both parties agree it is necessary to resolve identified information gaps. Issue 4. Contents of Certification Decisions: Ensure Requirements Do Not Marginalize Tribal Authorities or Indigenous Knowledge EPA proposes to define the required contents of certification decisions in regulatory text. The Tribe supports transparency but is concerned that overly rigid templates could be used to challenge tribal decisions that incorporate tribal legal authorities, treaty‑reserved rights, or Indigenous Knowledge in a manner appropriate to the Tribe’s governance and water protection responsibilities. Requested change: Treat decision‑content elements as minimums, allow incorporation by reference of supporting findings and technical documentation, and confirm that certifying authorities may include additional findings and conditions grounded in applicable tribal authority where relevant to assuring compliance with water quality requirements. Issue 5. Section 401(a)(2): Provide Sufficient Time for Meaningful Review of Downstream Impacts EPA proposes a 90‑day timeline for federal agencies to hold a public hearing and make a determination on a notified state’s objection under Section 401(a)(2). The Tribe is concerned that a rigid 90‑day timeframe may be insufficient for meaningful evaluation of downstream and interjurisdictional impacts affecting treaty resources. Requested change: Provide flexibility to extend the 90‑day period where needed to evaluate complex impacts, including impacts to treaty resources and downstream designated uses. The rule should be explicit in authorizing federal agencies to extend hearing and determination timelines at the request of affected Tribes or other governments (e.g., states) when additional time is needed for consultation. The 90-day timeframe should be viewed as the default timeframe, not a maximum. Issue 6. Preserving Tribal Sovereignty: Uphold Standalone Section 401 Pathways and Resist Erosion of Tribal Rights. EPA’s proposal would eliminate the stand-alone regulatory pathway for tribes to obtain Treatment in a Similar Manner as a State (TAS) for Clean Water Act Section 401 and instead require tribes to pursue TAS through the water quality standards process (Section 303(c)). Sault Tribe is concerned that this creates a significant barrier for Tribes that have strong water protection programs and treaty responsibilities but lack the staffing, time, or funding to complete a full water quality standards approval process simply to exercise Section 401 authority. Requested change: Retain a stand-alone Section 401 TAS pathway (including for the neighboring jurisdiction process) or establish a streamlined alternative that allows tribes to obtain and use Section 401 authority without requiring full water quality standards approval as a prerequisite. In closing, Section 401 is a crucial safeguard in the Clean Water Act, designed to prevent federally permitted activities from harming water resources and ecosystems. For the Sault Ste. Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians, this includes protecting treaty resources within the 1836 Treaty Ceded Territory that have been memorialized in the 2007 Inland Consent Decree and the 2023 Great Lakes Decree. The Tribe urges the EPA to revise the final rule to avoid restricting certifications to a discharge-only focus and to eliminate the one-size-fits- all checklist for certifying authorities. It’s important to retain necessary tools for creating a defensible record and to ensure decision-content requirements are minimum standards that respect tribal authority and Indigenous Knowledge. The process should allow for adjustments to certifications based on new information and must facilitate tribal exercise of Section 401 authority without imposing resource-intensive prerequisites. These changes would ensure that Section 401 remains protective, supports timely permitting, and safeguards waters and treaty resources. EPA should finalize a rule that enables Tribes to act as co-regulators and respects Tribal sovereignty and governing authority. Respectfully Submitted, Austin Lowes, Chairman Sault Ste. Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians
